name: Big Indian Wash
short_description: BLM public land in remote San Juan County offering agate and jasper in scenic desert wash terrain. Free rockhounding with 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Big Indian Wash is a scenic desert wash located on BLM public land in southeastern Utah's remote San Juan County. This location attracts rockhounders seeking colorful agates, jasper nodules, and chalcedony specimens in a relatively accessible wash environment. The wash cuts through typical high desert terrain, exposing geology rich with silica-based minerals deposited during ancient volcanic and sedimentary cycles.

The area is best explored by walking the wash bottom and surrounding benches where erosion naturally exposes mineral specimens. Colorful agate nodules and jasper fragments are commonly found loose in the wash sediments and along the banks. The terrain is moderately rugged but not technically difficult, making it suitable for rockhounders with basic field skills.

As BLM land, Big Indian Wash requires no permit and has no fees, but collectors must follow federal regulations: personal use only, maximum 25 pounds per day, and no commercial collection. The site offers solitude and genuine desert rockhounding experience typical of southeastern Utah's Bureau of Land Management holdings.

Water is not reliably available here, so bring adequate supplies. Cell service is spotty at best in this remote region. The wash can flash flood during monsoon season (July-September), so check weather before visiting during these months. Fall and winter offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.
history: Big Indian Wash lies within the Colorado Plateau province where Precambrian and Paleozoic sedimentary formations were uplifted and eroded over millions of years. Miocene-age volcanic activity in the region deposited silica-rich hydrothermal solutions that crystallized into agate nodules and jasper deposits now exposed by modern erosion in wash systems like this one. The wash itself represents ongoing drainage development through consolidated rock layers, naturally exhuming mineral specimens for surface collection.
tips: Search the wash bottom and cut banks where water erosion exposes fresh material and loose specimens collect in benches and side deposits. Look for colorful agate nodules ranging from golf-ball to fist-size, often showing red, orange, white, or banded patterns. Bring a 5-gallon bucket, small pry bar, and hand lens to inspect specimens; use the bar carefully to remove nodules from matrix without damaging them. Focus your search on the outer bends of the wash where water current concentrates heavier mineral material.
terrain_notes: Big Indian Wash sits at approximately 5,800 feet elevation in high desert terrain with scattered pinyon and juniper. The wash terrain is moderately steep with uneven footing, exposed bedrock, and loose cobbles. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas ranges from a few hundred feet to over a mile depending on route. Footing is rocky and requires sturdy boots; loose material can shift unexpectedly. No established trails; navigation by wash itself.
best_season: October - April. Desert heat becomes extreme May-September, and monsoon storms (July-September) create flash flood hazards in the wash. Spring winds are strong but temperatures are milder. Winter temperatures are cool but manageable for active rockhounding.
written_directions: From Blanding, take US 191 south approximately 25 miles toward Mexican Hat. Turn east on a marked BLM access road (Cow Canyon Road or similar local designation) and follow it for 8-12 miles to Big Indian Wash area. Roads are dirt and may be rough depending on recent weather. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Park at natural pullouts near the wash and hike to productive areas on foot. Confirm current access routes with Monticello BLM field office as road conditions and access points may change.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt roads, may have washboard and ruts. High-clearance vehicle recommended but standard passenger cars may access lower access points during dry conditions. Not suitable for low-clearance sedans.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along wash access roads. No developed parking lot. Park safely off the main track to avoid blocking traffic.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation. Surface collection only; do not dig into solid bedrock or remove material from cliff faces. Respect cultural resources and do not collect artifacts.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Surface collection only
No artifacts
nearest_services: Blanding (approximately 25 miles) has gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Mexican Hat (approximately 40 miles south) has limited services. Carry water and supplies for full day trips.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven footing, loose rocks, and no established trails. The wash bottom is navigable but requires careful footing on cobbles and exposed bedrock. Steep banks in places. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Recommended for rockhounders with basic scrambling ability and good physical conditioning.

faq.question: What types of agates and rocks can I find at Big Indian Wash in Utah?
faq.answer: Big Indian Wash is known for colorful agate nodules, jasper fragments, and chalcedony specimens. Expect red, orange, white, and banded agate in sizes ranging from golf-ball to fist-size, plus chert chips and jasper pieces in the wash sediments. The area's Miocene volcanic history created ideal conditions for silica mineral formation.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Big Indian Wash free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Big Indian Wash is free BLM public land with no permit required for recreational collecting. You can collect up to 25 pounds per day for personal use only. There are no fees or special applications needed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Big Indian Wash near Blanding?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Big Indian Wash. Desert heat becomes dangerous May-September, and monsoon storms July-September create flash flood risks in the wash. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and good collecting conditions.

faq.question: What vehicle and tools do I need for rockhounding at Big Indian Wash?
faq.answer: A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended for the unpaved dirt access roads. Essential tools include a 5-gallon bucket, small pry bar for specimen extraction, hand lens for inspection, sturdy hiking boots, and a backpack. Bring plenty of water and sun protection.

faq.question: Is Big Indian Wash beginner-friendly for new rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Big Indian Wash is suitable for beginners. Surface specimens are accessible from the wash, no digging is required, and the terrain, while rocky, does not demand technical climbing skills. Bring good boots and work at a comfortable pace. The free access and generous 25 lb limit make it ideal for learning.

faq.question: How far is Big Indian Wash from Blanding and what should I bring?
faq.answer: Big Indian Wash is approximately 25 miles south of Blanding via US 191 and local BLM roads. It's a remote location, so bring plenty of water (minimum 2 liters), lunch, sun protection, a headlamp, a first aid kit, and a charged vehicle battery. Cell service is spotty, so inform someone of your itinerary.
